scenario: The student plays everything at the same volume -- no contrast between the gentle opening melody and the louder middle section.
teacher_response: Think of the opening melody as a whisper - let your fingers barely kiss the keys, like you're sharing a secret. Then when we get to that stormy middle part, really lean into the keys with your whole arm and let the sound bloom out from the piano.

template.context: The student plays everything at the same volume -- no contrast between the gentle opening melody and the louder middle section.

passage_description: climactic passage in the middle section
scenario: The student rushes through the crescendo leading to the climax, arriving at forte too early and losing the gradual build.
teacher_response: Think of that crescendo like slowly turning up a dimmer switch rather than flipping on a light bulb - let each note add just a tiny layer of intensity so the forte feels like it's been earned when you finally reach the peak. Try stretching out that climb by imagining you're walking up a long, gradual hill instead of sprinting up stairs.

template.passage_description: climactic passage in the middle section
template.context: The student rushes through the crescendo leading to the climax, arriving at forte too early and losing the gradual build.

scenario: The student's pulse is uneven -- rushing through easy bars and slowing down at difficult ones.
teacher_response: Your hands are telling the story of what's coming next instead of staying in the present moment! Try counting out loud while you play, and when you feel a tricky part approaching, resist the urge to slow down by keeping your foot tapping that steady beat. Think of it like walking at the same pace whether the sidewalk is smooth or bumpy.

template.context: The student's pulse is uneven -- rushing through easy bars and slowing down at difficult ones.

scenario: The student plays with strict metronomic time. The melody needs rubato but they're afraid to bend the tempo.
teacher_response: Think of it like breathing - let the melody inhale on those rising phrases and exhale as it settles down. Try pulling back just a tiny bit on that high E-flat, like you're savoring a beautiful view, then let the phrase gently flow forward again.

template.context: The student plays with strict metronomic time. The melody needs rubato but they're afraid to bend the tempo.

scenario: The student holds the sustain pedal down for entire phrases, creating harmonic blur when the bass changes.
teacher_response: Your pedal is creating a muddy soup down there - you need to clear it each time the bass note changes. Try lifting your right foot just as your thumb plays the new bass note, then press it back down immediately to catch the fresh harmony.

template.context: The student holds the sustain pedal down for entire phrases, creating harmonic blur when the bass changes.

scenario: The pedaling is technically correct but mechanical -- changing on every beat rather than following the musical phrase.
teacher_response: Think of the pedal as breathing with the melody rather than just marking time - let those longer phrases float on one pedal change, especially when the melody is spinning out those beautiful legato lines. Try holding the pedal through the entire first part of each phrase and only change when the harmony actually shifts, so the waltz can really sing instead of chopping along.

template.context: The pedaling is technically correct but mechanical -- changing on every beat rather than following the musical phrase.

scenario: The student's staccato sounds heavy and clunky -- more like dropping the keys than releasing them.
teacher_response: Think of your fingers as little dancers bouncing lightly off a hot stove - you want to pull away quickly, not press down and lift heavily. Try using just your wrist to help snap your fingers up and off the keys, like you're flicking water droplets from your fingertips.

template.context: The student's staccato sounds heavy and clunky -- more like dropping the keys than releasing them.

scenario: All notes are played with identical touch. Bach's counterpoint needs varying articulation to distinguish the voices.
teacher_response: Think of your right and left hands as having different personalities in conversation - try making the ascending runs more legato and flowing, like they're asking a question, while the descending patterns get a slightly more detached, crisp touch, as if they're answering back. Let your wrists stay flexible so each voice can "speak" with its own character rather than everything sounding like the same monotone voice.

template.context: All notes are played with identical touch. Bach's counterpoint needs varying articulation to distinguish the voices.

piece: Prelude in C Major, WTC I
scenario: The student plays each bar as an isolated unit. There's no sense of larger phrase direction across multiple bars.
teacher_response: Think of your right hand as drawing a long, gentle curve in the air that spans across several bars - don't let your wrist drop at every barline like you're chopping vegetables. Try playing four bars in one breath, letting the melody notes on top float and connect like they're singing one continuous line.

template.piece: Prelude in C Major, WTC I
template.context: The student plays each bar as an isolated unit. There's no sense of larger phrase direction across multiple bars.

scenario: The melody is technically smooth but flat -- no sense of where the phrase is going, no arrival point, no breathing between phrases.
teacher_response: Think of this melody like you're singing a long, beautiful sentence - you need to know where you're heading and take a breath at the comma. Try shaping each phrase like a gentle hill, where your wrist lifts slightly as you climb toward the peak notes, then releases as the phrase settles down.

template.context: The melody is technically smooth but flat -- no sense of where the phrase is going, no arrival point, no breathing between phrases.

scenario: The student is playing the notes correctly but hasn't thought about what the music expresses. It sounds like an exercise.
teacher_response: Think of this melody as a gentle sigh - let your wrist float down with the weight of sadness on those long notes, like you're tracing teardrops in the air. You're getting all the right notes, which is wonderful, so now let's make them tell their story by imagining you're singing this tune to someone you miss.

template.context: The student is playing the notes correctly but hasn't thought about what the music expresses. It sounds like an exercise.

scenario: The right hand melody is well-voiced but the left hand chromatic descent -- which drives the emotional tension -- is barely audible.
teacher_response: Let that left hand chromatic line breathe and sing - it's the heartbeat of this prelude, so bring it forward like you're drawing a bow across violin strings. Try thinking of your right hand melody as floating on top while your left hand thumb walks down those chromatic steps with real weight and intention. The melody can't carry all the emotion alone - that descending line is what makes our hearts sink.

template.context: The right hand melody is well-voiced but the left hand chromatic descent -- which drives the emotional tension -- is barely audible.

piece: Ravel Piano Concerto in G, 2nd movement
scenario: The student's pianissimo is beautiful but static. Ravel's long melodic line needs micro-dynamic shaping within the pp.
teacher_response: Think of your pianissimo as a silk ribbon floating in a gentle breeze—it still needs to breathe and curve. Try adding tiny swells within each phrase, like you're painting with watercolors where some areas are just a shade deeper than others, so the melody can sing even at this whisper-soft level.

template.piece: Ravel Piano Concerto in G, 2nd movement
template.context: The student's pianissimo is beautiful but static. Ravel's long melodic line needs micro-dynamic shaping within the pp.

scenario: The student is using full pedal changes where half-pedal and flutter techniques would create the water-like shimmer Debussy intended.
teacher_response: Try thinking of your foot as if you're dipping your toe into water rather than diving in completely - use just enough pedal to let those harmonies blur together like ripples, then flutter your ankle gently to catch the new colors without washing away the old ones. Your pedal should breathe with the music, creating that liquid shimmer where one reflection melts into the next.

template.context: The student is using full pedal changes where half-pedal and flutter techniques would create the water-like shimmer Debussy intended.

scenario: The ornaments are rhythmically precise but sound academic. The sarabande pulse needs to breathe around the ornaments, not accommodate them mechanically.
teacher_response: Let the sarabande's heartbeat stay strong underneath—think of the ornaments as jewelry draped over the melody, not surgical insertions that stop the flow. Try conducting the underlying triple pulse with your body while you play, so those grace notes can float and settle naturally into the rhythm instead of landing with mathematical precision.

template.context: The ornaments are rhythmically precise but sound academic. The sarabande pulse needs to breathe around the ornaments, not accommodate them mechanically.

scenario: The chords are powerful but every attack sounds the same. The top voice of each chord should project above the rest.
teacher_response: Think of your right hand like a spotlight operator - while your whole hand drops into those massive chords with full weight, your pinky needs to punch through like it's breaking glass while the other fingers just land solidly. Try playing the top notes alone first with that piercing attack, then gradually add the lower voices underneath, keeping that knife-edge clarity in the melody line even as you thunder through the full chords.

template.context: The chords are powerful but every attack sounds the same. The top voice of each chord should project above the rest.

scenario: The student manages the complex counterpoint technically but each voice has the same phrasing. The soprano line needs independent shaping from the inner voices.
teacher_response: Think of your right hand as conducting two different conversations at once - let your thumb and pinky have completely different breathing patterns, like one person speaking in short, urgent phrases while the other takes long, sweeping sentences. Try isolating just the soprano line first and really exaggerate its rise and fall, then keep that singer alive in your pinky while you add back the more steady, underlying voices with your other fingers.

template.context: The student manages the complex counterpoint technically but each voice has the same phrasing. The soprano line needs independent shaping from the inner voices.

scenario: The Arietta is played beautifully but the transition into the first variation doesn't feel like a natural evolution -- there's a disconnect between the theme's simplicity and the variation's energy.
teacher_response: Think of the first variation as the Arietta stretching and waking up rather than suddenly jumping out of bed - let the simple melody gradually discover its own inner restlessness. Try carrying the theme's singing quality right into those first sixteenth notes, so they feel like the same voice just becoming more animated, not a completely different character taking over.

template.context: The Arietta is played beautifully but the transition into the first variation doesn't feel like a natural evolution -- there's a disconnect between the theme's simplicity and the variation's energy.

scenario: The student has been struggling with this piece for weeks but today the opening section finally has a real sense of line and direction.
teacher_response: Beautiful! I can hear you breathing with the music now - that long phrase is finally singing instead of just being a collection of notes. Keep trusting that natural ebb and flow you found, like you're drawing one continuous line with your whole arm.

template.context: The student has been struggling with this piece for weeks but today the opening section finally has a real sense of line and direction.

scenario: The student is maintaining a consistent pp throughout with gentle dynamic shaping. For a beginner, this is unusual control.
teacher_response: What beautiful control you have over those soft dynamics - you're really capturing that dreamy, floating quality Satie was after! Your fingers are finding just the right gentle weight to let each note breathe without disturbing the peaceful atmosphere. Keep trusting that delicate touch - you're playing with the sensitivity of someone much more advanced.

template.context: The student is maintaining a consistent pp throughout with gentle dynamic shaping. For a beginner, this is unusual control.
